title: Anthropic co-founder shares the Vatican stage for the AI encyclical
subtitle: Chris Olah helped present Magnifica Humanitas alongside Pope Leo XIV, a pairing that drew both praise and criticism inside the Church and the industry.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: When Pope Leo XIV presented Magnifica Humanitas on May 25, he broke with custom by personally introducing the encyclical rather than handing the role to a cardinal — and he did it alongside Chris Olah, the 33-year-old co-founder of Anthropic, the company behind the Claude models. Olah, who is not religious, used his remarks to call for an ongoing ethics partnership between the Church and the technology industry. He acknowledged that AI development 'operates inside a set of incentives and constraints that can sometimes conflict with doing the right thing,' and said the world needs 'religious communities, civil society, scholars, governments — to do what His Holiness has done here: to take this seriously, to look closely, and to push events in a better direction.'

The pairing matters because it is unusual on both sides. For the Vatican, inviting a frontier-lab founder to help launch a document that criticizes the concentration of AI power is a calculated bet that engagement beats condemnation. For Anthropic, standing next to a pope who is calling for stricter regulation of exactly its industry is a striking piece of positioning for a company that markets itself as the safety-conscious lab. Critics inside the Church argued the appearance amounted to a papal stamp of approval for one company. Others read it more narrowly — as recognition of Anthropic's prominence, closer to a papal audience with a head of state than an endorsement.

The optics are complicated by where Anthropic actually sits with governments right now. The company is in a public legal fight with the Trump administration after the Department of Defense labeled it a 'supply chain risk' and federal agencies were ordered to stop using its technology — a dispute that traces back to Anthropic refusing to grant the military unrestricted access to its models. So the lab being held up at the Vatican as a model of 'goodwill and integrity' is, at the same moment, being treated by its own government as a national-security problem. That tension is the real story: there is no settled consensus on whether companies like Anthropic are part of the solution or part of the risk.

Takeaway for learners: watch where AI companies choose to show up, not just what they ship. A founder on a Vatican stage, in a congressional hearing, or at a standards body is making a bet about how this technology will be governed and who gets to shape the rules. If you are entering this field, the ability to operate in those rooms — to translate between engineers, ethicists, and regulators — is becoming as valuable as the ability to train a model. The people who can hold a credible conversation about both the math and the consequences will have outsized influence over what gets built.

sources.title: Pope Leo called AI an 'instrument of domination, exclusion and death.' Anthropic was in the room — Fortune
